---
title: "R statistical analysis"
author: "Dominik Truchly"
date: "2024-12-17"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)
```

```{r}
library("dplyr")
library("tidyr")
library("ggplot2")
library("Hmisc")		  #	rcorr
library("ppcor")		  #	pcor
library("GGally")     # ggpairs
library("ggcorrplot") # ggcorrplot
library("gridExtra")  # grid.arrange
library("ggplot2")
library("agricolae")
library("car")
library("nortest")

library(ggcorrplot)
```


# TASK 1
```{r}
# Load data
ChildCarSeats <- read.csv("data/ChildCarSeats.csv")

head(dt)

```
```{r}
# Oneway ANOVA
anova_model <- aov(Sales ~ Advert, data = ChildCarSeats)

summary(anova_model)
cat("P-value is 2e-16, which is very small, so we reject H0.")
cat("Conclusion: advertisements have a statistically significant effect on sales.")
```

```{r}
leveneTest(Sales ~ Advert, data = ChildCarSeats)
cat("P-value is 0.44 (> 0.05), variances are homogeneous (ANOVA assumption satisfied).")
```
```{r}
residuals <- residuals(anova_model)
shapiro.test(residuals)
cat("P-value is 0.37 (> 0.05), residuals follow a normal distribution (ANOVA assumption satisfied).")

```
```{r}
# Boxplot for visualization
ggplot(ChildCarSeats, aes(x = Advert, y = Sales, fill = Advert)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Sales by Advertisement Type", y = "Sales", x = "Advertisement Type") +
  theme_minimal()
```
```{r}
# Tukey's HSD test
tukey_results <- TukeyHSD(anova_model)
print(tukey_results)
cat("
Extra advertisement leads to significantly higher sales than Common (mean difference: 290.74).
No advertisement results in significantly lower sales than Common (−178.37).
No advertisement yields the lowest sales, with a large gap compared to Extra (−469.11).")

```
# TASK 2
```{r}
# Scatterplot matrix for Sales, Price, CompPrice
ggpairs(ChildCarSeats[, c("Sales", "Price", "CompPrice")],
        title = "Scatterplot Matrix for Sales, Price, and CompPrice",
        aes(color = "blue", alpha = 0.5))

```

```{r}
# Calculate Pearson correlation matrix
cor_matrix <- cor(ChildCarSeats[, c("Sales", "Price", "CompPrice")], method = "pearson")
print(cor_matrix)
```
```{r}
# Plot correlation matrix
plot11 <- ggcorrplot(cor_matrix, 
                     title = "Pearson's Correlations", 
                     lab = TRUE, 
                     type = "full", 
                     method = "square", 
                     outline.color = "white", 
                     show.legend = FALSE)
print(plot11)
# Values closer to 1 indicate a strong positive relationship, -1 a strong negative one, and 0 no relationship
```


```{r}
# Calculate variance-covariance matrix
var_cov_matrix <- cov(ChildCarSeats[, c("Sales", "Price", "CompPrice")])
print(var_cov_matrix)
cat("Covariance indicates that Price and Sales are inversely proportional, while Sales and CompPrice have a weak positive relationship.")
```

```{r}
# Calculate sample standard deviations
sample_sd <- sqrt(diag(var_cov_matrix))
names(sample_sd) <- c("Sales", "Price", "CompPrice")
print(sample_sd)
cat("Sales shows the highest variability, while Price and CompPrice have low variability.")
```

# TASK 3
```{r}
ChildCarSeats <- read.csv("data/ChildCarSeats.csv")

head(ChildCarSeats)
```
```{r}
# Contingency table
table_expensive_city <- table(ChildCarSeats$Expensive, ChildCarSeats$City)
print(table_expensive_city)
```

```{r}
# Calculate odds ratio
odds_ratio <- (table_expensive_city[2, 2] / table_expensive_city[1, 2]) / 
              (table_expensive_city[2, 1] / table_expensive_city[1, 1])
print(paste("odds ratio: ", odds_ratio))
```

```{r}
# Calculate the standard error for the log(odds ratio)
se_log_or <- sqrt(1/table_expensive_city[1, 1] + 1/table_expensive_city[2, 1] + 
                  1/table_expensive_city[1, 2] + 1/table_expensive_city[2, 2])

# Calculate the log of the odds ratio
log_or <- log(odds_ratio)
conf_int_log_or <- c(log_or - 1.96 * se_log_or, log_or + 1.96 * se_log_or)
conf_int_or <- exp(conf_int_log_or)
print(paste("95% Confidence Interval odds ratio: [", conf_int_or[1], ", ", conf_int_or[2], "]", sep = ""))
```
```{r}
cat("The CI [0.6379, 1.5069] spans 1, meaning there is no statistically significant association between City and Expensive.")
```


```{r}
# Mosaic plot for Expensive vs City
mosaicplot(table_expensive_city, main = "Mosaic Plot Expensive vs City", 
           color = c("lightblue", "lightgreen"), 
           xlab = "City", ylab = "Expensive")
```

# TASK 4
```{r}
ChildCarSeats <- read.csv("data/ChildCarSeats.csv")

# Build linear regression model
model <- lm(Sales ~ Price + City, data = ChildCarSeats)
summary(model)
```
```{r}
cat("
The model shows that Price has a significant negative effect on Sales (as price increases, sales decrease).
City does not have a significant effect on Sales (high p-value: 0.903).
The model is statistically significant but explains only ~19.4% of the variability in sales (R-squared).")
```

```{r}
# Plot the model
ggplot(ChildCarSeats, aes(x = Price, y = Sales, color = City)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "blue") +
  labs(title = "Regression of Sales on Price and City",
       x = "Price", y = "Sales") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
par(mfrow = c(2, 2))
plot(model) 
```
```{r}
cat("
Residuals vs Fitted: ideally should show a random scatter (residuals not systematically related to fitted values).

QQ Residuals: if residuals are normally distributed, points should roughly follow the diagonal line.

Scale-Location: random scatter with no clear pattern (equal variance of residuals across all levels of predictors).

Residuals vs Leverage: highlights points that are outliers or have high leverage."
)
```

```{r}
# Coefficient of multiple correlation between the response and predictors
# Value taken from model summary
r_squared <- summary(model)$r.squared
cat("R-squared: ", r_squared, "\n")
```
```{r}
# value of Sales for Price = 150 and City = "Yes"
new_data <- data.frame(Price = 150, City = "Yes")
pred_sales <- predict(model, new_data, interval = "confidence", level = 0.95)
cat("Predicted Sales with 95% confidence interval: \n")
print(pred_sales)
```
```{r}
# Sales with 95% prediction interval for Price = 150 and City = "Yes"
pred_sales_with_PI <- predict(model, new_data, interval = "prediction", level = 0.95)
cat("Predicted Sales with 95% prediction interval: \n")
print(pred_sales_with_PI)
```
```{r}
cat("The significant Pearson correlation between Sales and Price in Task 2 (-0.4449507) corresponds to the negative regression coefficient for Price, which has a p-value < 2e-16.")
```
# TASK 5
```{r}
ChildCarSeats <- read.csv("data/ChildCarSeats.csv")

# Build logistic regression model
ChildCarSeats$Expensive <- ifelse(ChildCarSeats$Expensive == "Yes", 1, 0)

logit_model <- glm(Expensive ~ Sales, data = ChildCarSeats, family = "binomial")

summary(logit_model)
```
```{r}
cat("The model indicates that Sales is significantly associated with the probability of an item being labelled Expensive (p-value < 2e-16). The negative coefficient means that as sales increase, the probability of the car seat being expensive decreases.")
```

```{r}
# Plot logistic regression curve
sales_range <- seq(min(ChildCarSeats$Sales), max(ChildCarSeats$Sales), length.out = 100)

predicted_probabilities <- predict(logit_model, newdata = data.frame(Sales = sales_range), type = "response")

plot_data <- data.frame(Sales = sales_range, Predicted_Prob = predicted_probabilities)
ggplot(ChildCarSeats, aes(x = Sales, y = Expensive)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_line(data = plot_data, aes(x = Sales, y = Predicted_Prob), color = "blue") +
  labs(x = "Sales", y = "Probability of Expensive = Yes", title = "Logistic Regression: Sales vs. Probability of Expensive") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
# Calculate probability of Expensive == "Yes" when Sales = 1000
sales_value <- 1000

coefficients <- coef(logit_model)

log_odds <- coefficients[1] + coefficients[2] * sales_value

probability <- 1 / (1 + exp(-log_odds))

se <- sqrt(diag(vcov(logit_model)))
lower_log_odds <- log_odds - 1.96 * se[2] * sales_value
upper_log_odds <- log_odds + 1.96 * se[2] * sales_value
lower_prob <- 1 / (1 + exp(-lower_log_odds))
upper_prob <- 1 / (1 + exp(-upper_log_odds))

cat("Probability of Expensive == 'Yes' when Sales = 1000: ", probability, "\n")
cat("95% Confidence Interval for Probability: [", lower_prob, ", ", upper_prob, "]\n")
```
# TASK 6
```{r}
ChildCarSeats <- read.csv("data/ChildCarSeats.csv")

# Boxplot for Price and CompPrice
ggplot(ChildCarSeats, aes(x = factor(0), y = Price)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue") +
  geom_boxplot(data = ChildCarSeats, aes(x = factor(1), y = CompPrice), fill = "lightgreen") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = c("Price", "CompPrice")) +
  ylab("Price / CompPrice") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
# Wilcoxon signed-rank test to compare medians of 
wilcox_test <- wilcox.test(ChildCarSeats$Price, ChildCarSeats$CompPrice, alternative = "less")
print(wilcox_test)
```
```{r}
cat("The p-value is very small (we reject the null hypothesis). Conclusion: the median of Price is significantly smaller than the median of CompPrice, as the boxplot also suggests.")
```
